content:
First, observe that any contiguous block of 1’s of length j contributes the integer  
\[v_j=\underbrace{111\cdots1}_{j\text{ times}}=\frac{10^j-1}9\,. \]  
Since \(v_4=1111>1000\), only blocks of length \(1,2,3\) can occur.  If we use  
\(m_1\) blocks of “1”, \(m_2\) blocks of “11”, and \(m_3\) blocks of “111”,  
then the total is  
\[
m_1\cdot1\;+\;m_2\cdot11\;+\;m_3\cdot111 \;=\;1000,
\]  
and the total length of the original string is  
\[
n \;=\;m_1\cdot1 \;+\;m_2\cdot2 \;+\;m_3\cdot3.
\]  
From the first equation \(m_1=1000-11m_2-111m_3\), so
\[
n \;=\;(1000-11m_2-111m_3)+2m_2+3m_3
    \;=\;1000-9m_2-108m_3.
\]
Thus 
\[
n=1000-9\,(m_2+12\,m_3)\,.
\]
The non‐negativity condition \(m_1\ge0\) is
\[
11m_2+111m_3\le1000\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
m_2\le\Big\lfloor\frac{1000-111m_3}{11}\Big\rfloor,
\]
with \(m_3=0,1,2,\dots,9\).  One checks that the quantity
\[
k'=m_2+12m_3
\]
runs exactly through all integers \(0,1,2,\dots,106\) and also \(108\) (but not 107).  Hence
\[
n=1000-9k'
\]
takes exactly \(108\) distinct positive values.  Therefore
\[
a=108,
\]
and the sum of the digits of \(a\) is
\[
1+0+8=9.
\]
\boxed{9}

\[
m_1\cdot1\;+\;m_2\cdot11\;+\;m_3\cdot111 \;=\;1000,

\[
